metadata.author: LivingPatch Contributors
metadata.description: Core LivingPatch dataset: Northeast Pennsylvania species, taxonomic groups, and ecological relationships

species.functional_description: Largest woodpecker in PA. Black with red crest. Excavates large rectangular holes in dead trees searching for carpenter ants, creating cavities used by owls, kestrels, and flying squirrels.
species.keystone_description: Primary cavity excavator. Abandoned cavities used by Great Horned Owl, American Kestrel, Wood Duck, and flying squirrels. Creates snags that structure the entire forest bird community.

species.functional_description: Large broad-winged hawk with rusty-red tail. Hunts from elevated perches and open soaring. Eats mostly small mammals; controls rodent and squirrel populations.
species.keystone_description: Apex daytime predator of open habitats. Controls gray squirrel, vole, and cottontail populations. Major fall migration corridor at Kittatinny Ridge (Hawk Mountain).

image.author: Becky Matsubara from El Sobrante, California

species.functional_description: Largest owl in PA. Earlike tufts, yellow eyes. Hunts at night. Earliest-nesting PA bird — eggs laid January–February. Uniquely tolerates skunk spray.
species.keystone_description: Apex nocturnal predator. Controls rabbits, squirrels, skunks, and even other raptors. Depends on Pileated Woodpecker cavities and abandoned Red-tailed Hawk nests.

species.functional_description: Sleek crested bird with yellow tail tip, red wax wing spots. One of PA's latest-nesting birds — timed to summer fruit peak. Highly frugivorous; major seed disperser of native shrubs.
Small warbler, male with black mask. Sings witchety-witchety-witch. Nests low in marsh vegetation. Host to Brown-headed Cowbird brood parasitism. Eats insects including butterflies.
Smallest falcon, robin-sized. Hovers over open fields. Eats grasshoppers in summer, voles year-round. Declining in PA due to farmland loss and Cooper's Hawk predation pressure. Uses nest boxes.
Small thrush with electric blue back, rusty breast. Cavity nester; uses nest boxes. Eats insects in summer, native berries in winter. Secondary cavity user — depends on woodpecker holes.
Smallest PA woodpecker. Black-and-white with small bill. Joins mixed winter flocks. Key winter forager: excavates goldenrod galls to eat Eurosta gall fly larvae inside — a critical food-web link.

Only breeding hummingbird in eastern NA. Arrives late April coinciding with Eastern Columbine bloom. Primary pollinator of red tubular native flowers. Also eats insects for protein; uses Yellow-bellied Sapsucker sap wells in spring.

Largest heron in NA — 4 ft tall. Stands motionless in shallows then strikes. Eats fish, frogs, snakes, rodents. Nests colonially in heronries of up to 500 nests. Strongly associated with beaver ponds in PA.
Largest NA rodent. Fells trees, builds dams, creates ponds. Transforms streams into wetland habitat that supports herons, ducks, frogs, and countless other species. Kits born April–June in lodge.
species.keystone_description: Single most powerful landscape engineer in NE PA. Beaver ponds create habitat for Great Blue Heron, Wood Duck, Hooded Merganser, American Bullfrog, Spotted Salamander, painted turtles, and muskrat. Beaver-killed snags multiply cavity supply for Pileated Woodpecker.

species.functional_description: Gray bushy-tailed rodent. Buries acorns in scatter-hoards, forgetting many — making it a critical oak regenerator alongside Blue Jays. Prefers White Oak acorns (lower tannins). Active year-round; no hibernation.

Most abundant large mammal in PA. Overabundant in absence of natural predators. Suppresses oak seedlings, eliminates native wildflowers (Trillium, orchids), and facilitates invasive plants. Fawns born (spotted) late May–June.

Large tree with lobed leaves and rounded acorns. Preferred by wildlife over red oak — lower tannins make acorns palatable immediately on drop. Acorns germinate in fall. Supports 557 Lepidoptera species (genus Quercus). Lives 200+ years.
species.keystone_description: Hosts more wildlife than almost any other native tree. Acorns feed squirrels, deer, turkey, jays, woodpeckers, black bear. Leaves support 557 Lepidoptera caterpillar species. Long-lived — a single mature white oak feeds dozens of species.

species.functional_description: Large oak with bristle-tipped leaf lobes and red fall color. Acorns take 2 years to mature, ripen in fall and germinate the following spring — different from White Oak. Same 557-species Lepidoptera support as other native oaks.
species.keystone_description: Co-dominant with White Oak in NE PA upland forests. Acorns high-tannin — cached by squirrels longer than white oak acorns. Same broad wildlife support as Q. alba.

species.functional_description: Fast-growing tree of streambanks and floodplains. March–April catkins are critical early nectar/pollen for emerging bumble bee queens and Andrena bees. Primary food tree of Beaver. Roots stabilize eroding banks. Hosts 289+ Lepidoptera species.

Tall perennial with pink-purple globe flowers and milky toxic sap. Sole host for Monarch caterpillars (obligate at genus). Also hosts Red Milkweed Beetle, Large Milkweed Bug, and Milkweed Tussock Moth — all sequester its cardenolide toxins for self-defense.
species.keystone_description: Anchor of the milkweed guild. Monarch cannot breed without it. Also essential for Red Milkweed Beetle (root larvae obligate), Large Milkweed Bug (seed specialist), Milkweed Tussock Moth, and 11 additional Asclepias-specialist insects. Critical fall nectar source.

species.functional_description: Bright orange wildflower of dry, sandy soils. Unlike other milkweeds, has no milky sap. Host for Monarch caterpillars. Pollinated by butterflies, bumble bees, and hummingbirds. Seeds wind-dispersed via silky coma.

Pink-flowered milkweed of wet soils and stream edges. Host for Monarch caterpillars. Primary host for Swamp Milkweed Leaf Beetle. Blooms July–August, providing late summer nectar. Co-occurs with Joe-Pye weed, cardinal flower, blue vervain.

image.author: The original uploader was Teune at English Wikipedia.

species.functional_description: Yellow-flowered native perennials — PA has 15+ species. NOT the cause of hay fever (ragweed is). Blooms Aug–Oct, providing the most important late-season nectar in NE PA. Host for Eurosta gall fly (ball galls on stems, conspicuous in winter), whose larvae Downy Woodpeckers excavate. Supports 43 specialist bee species.
species.keystone_description: Dominant late-season nectar source for migrating Monarchs, 43 specialist native bee species, bumble bee gynes (fall queens building fat reserves), and dozens of generalist pollinators. Eurosta gall larvae are critical winter food for Downy Woodpeckers and Black-capped Chickadees.

species.functional_description: Red and yellow tubular flowers on rocky woodland slopes. Blooms late April–May — timed with Ruby-throated Hummingbird arrival. Primary pollinator is the hummingbird; bumble bees also access nectar. Host for Columbine Duskywing butterfly and Columbine Leafminer fly.

Fragrant understory shrub. Earliest woody bloom in PA forests (March–April), providing critical nectar before other plants open. Obligate host for Spicebush Swallowtail. Dioecious — only female plants produce the high-fat red berries that fuel fall migrating thrushes.

Medium tree — backbone of the Allegheny Plateau timber industry. Hosts 456 Lepidoptera species (genus Prunus). Small dark cherries in August eaten by 30+ bird species and many mammals. Primary PA host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail and Eastern Tent Caterpillar.
species.keystone_description: Second only to oaks in total Lepidoptera support (456 species). Cherries fuel migrant thrushes, waxwings, and many other birds. Primary host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail in PA. Eastern Tent Caterpillar — conspicuous spring indicator — depends on it.

species.functional_description: Orange and black butterfly toxic to most predators. Caterpillars obligate on genus Asclepias. 2–3 summer breeding generations in NE PA (June–September). Fall 'Methuselah' generation migrates 3,000+ miles to Mexico. Adults fuel on Goldenrod and asters during fall migration.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Tiny cream egg on underside of milkweed leaf

life_stages.name: Caterpillar
life_stages.description: Yellow, black, and white striped; feeds only on milkweed

life_stages.name: Chrysalis
life_stages.description: Jade green with gold spots; hangs from milkweed or nearby surface

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Orange and black; look near milkweed and goldenrod

species.keystone_description: Obligate on milkweed — cannot breed without genus Asclepias. Pollinates milkweed and goldenrod. Iconic indicator of agricultural landscape health.

species.functional_description: Black butterfly with yellow spots, red eyespots on hindwing. Larvae obligate at Apiaceae family (carrot family): native Golden Alexanders, water hemlock, water parsnip; also Queen Anne's lace, dill, parsley. Young larvae mimic bird droppings. 2 broods in PA.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Yellow sphere on carrot-family plant

life_stages.name: Caterpillar
life_stages.description: Green with black bands and yellow dots on carrot-family foliage

life_stages.name: Chrysalis
life_stages.description: Green or brown; overwinters

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Black with yellow spots

species.functional_description: Large yellow-and-black striped butterfly. Primary PA host is Black Cherry; also uses tulip tree, ash, willow, birch, and others — broad generalist. Late-instar caterpillars have large false eyespots mimicking a snake's head. Males gather at mud puddles. 2 broods in PA.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Green sphere on host plant leaf

life_stages.name: Caterpillar
life_stages.description: Green with snake-eye eyespots; rests in rolled leaf

life_stages.name: Chrysalis
life_stages.description: Mottled brown; overwinters

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Large tiger-striped butterfly; dark-form females mimic Pipevine Swallowtail

species.functional_description: Iridescent blue-green butterfly obligate on Lauraceae family — primarily Spicebush and Sassafras in PA. Caterpillars build silk-sealed leaf shelters on host plant (unfold curled leaves to find them). Late instars have spectacular false snake eyespots. Mimics toxic Pipevine Swallowtail as adult.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: On Spicebush or Sassafras leaf

life_stages.name: Caterpillar
life_stages.description: Green with huge eyespots; inside silk-sealed folded leaf

life_stages.name: Chrysalis
life_stages.description: Brown or green; overwinters

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Blue-green iridescence on hindwing

species.functional_description: Bright red-and-black longhorn beetle obligate on milkweed. Adults feed on foliage and flowers; uniquely, larvae develop in milkweed roots (hidden but critical). Sequesters cardenolide toxins — toxic and avoided by most birds. Adults active June–August on milkweed plants.

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Red with black spots on milkweed foliage and flowers

life_stages.name: Larva
life_stages.description: In milkweed roots — not visible without digging

species.functional_description: Orange-and-black bug specialist on milkweed seeds. Sequesters cardenolide toxins — few predators. NE PA populations are migratory — cannot overwinter here; recolonize from southern states each summer. Nymphs form conspicuous red-and-black clusters on milkweed pods July–October.

life_stages.name: Nymph
life_stages.description: Red and black; gregarious clusters on milkweed pods

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Orange and black X-pattern; on milkweed seeds and pods

species.functional_description: Fuzzy native bees — PA has 7+ resident species including Bombus impatiens (most common) and B. bimaculatus (earliest, late March). Only bumble bees can 'buzz pollinate' — vibrating to release pollen from blueberry, tomato, and other flowers. Queens overwinter in soil; workers and males die at frost. 43 specialist goldenrod bee species in Solidago genus.

life_stages.name: Queen
life_stages.description: Large; emerges from soil in early spring (March–April)

life_stages.name: Worker
life_stages.description: Medium; foraging at flowers

life_stages.name: New queen/male
life_stages.description: New queens (fat, large) mating in late summer

species.keystone_description: Essential pollinators of milkweed, columbine, goldenrod, native blueberries, and 100+ other plants. Primary pollinators of Common Milkweed (produce 8x more seed pods than nocturnal moths). Buzz pollination is unique among bees. Black Willow catkins are critical first-of-spring food.

species.functional_description: Largest NA frog. Native to NE PA (invasive only in western US). Tadpoles take 1–2 winters to metamorphose — a rare multi-year aquatic life stage. Adults eat anything they can catch: insects, crayfish, fish, smaller frogs, small snakes. Deep jug-o-rum call carries long distances.

life_stages.name: Egg mass
life_stages.description: Floating film of up to 20,000 eggs in June–July

life_stages.name: Tadpole
life_stages.description: Large; dark; in ponds and beaver impoundments — overwinters 1-2 years

life_stages.name: Froglet
life_stages.description: Emerging from water with tail stub; July–September of year 2 or 3

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Large green-brown frog with round eardrum larger than eye (male)

species.functional_description: Most colorful NA duck. Males iridescent green head, females brown with white teardrop eye-patch. Nests in cavities — depends on large cavities like those excavated by Pileated Woodpeckers or created by beavers. Perches on branches.

Nocturnal gliding squirrel. Stretches patagium (skin between limbs) to glide up to 150 feet. Nests in cavities created by Pileated Woodpeckers. Active at night hunting seeds, nuts, fungi, and insects.

image.author: (c) Louis Imbeau, some rights reserved (CC BY)

species.functional_description: Social caterpillars that build conspicuous silk tents in tree crotches during spring. Silk tents attract parasitoids and predatory wasps. Mass outbreaks occur cyclically (every 10-12 years). Important food source for birds.

Brilliant red male with black face mask; duller tan female. Thick conical bill adapted for cracking seeds. Year-round resident eating primarily seeds from grasses, forbs, and shrubs; supplements with insects during breeding season. Males sing year-round from exposed perches. Strong pair bond; both parents feed fledglings.

Striking blue, white, and black jay with prominent crest. Loud, harsh calls and excellent mimicry of hawk calls. Caches 3,000–5,000 acorns per fall — carrying up to 5 in its expandable throat pouch, transporting them up to 1 mile from source trees. In fragmented NE PA where forest patches are isolated by farms, roads, and development, blue jays are the primary mechanism connecting and regenerating oak stands. Also eats insects, eggs, and nestlings.
species.keystone_description: The keystone acorn disperser of eastern North America. Each Blue Jay caches 3,000–5,000 acorns per fall, carrying up to 5 in its expandable throat pouch and transporting them up to 1 mile — far beyond squirrel dispersal range. Responsible for rapid post-glacial oak reforestation. In today’s fragmented landscape, blue jays are the primary species moving oak seeds across farm fields, roads, and development gaps to establish new forest patches (Pesendorfer et al. 2016, Cornell Lab).

species.functional_description: Small, sleek, gray and white nuthatch that forages headfirst down tree trunks probing bark crevices for insects and spiders. Males have black caps; females gray. Acrobatic climber moving in all directions unlike other songbirds. Wedges sunflower seeds in bark crevices and hammers them open. Year-round resident that supplements winter diet with cached seeds.

Crested flycatcher with yellowish belly and russet tail. Hunts flying insects from exposed perches using aerial sallies — sallying up to 100 feet to catch flying insects in mid-air. Loud, sharp 'wheep' call. Nests in tree cavities, often using shed snake skin as lining material — function unknown but possibly deters predators. Migratory; arrives late April, departs by early September.

Common grasshoppers widespread in open habitats across PA. Short-horned locusts that jump using powerful hind legs. Males produce loud chirping songs by rubbing wings together to attract females. Important protein source for birds, reptiles, and small mammals. Nymphs and adults eat grasses, forbs, and crops. Peak abundance mid-summer through fall.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Laid in soil in late summer; overwinters

life_stages.name: Nymph
life_stages.description: Multiple instars; wingless; grow through spring and summer

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Winged; active in fields; calling and singing

species.functional_description: Small bark beetles that tunnel through inner bark and sapwood of dead and dying pine, spruce, and fir trees. Adults and larvae create distinctive 'engraved' gallery patterns under bark. Important decomposers of dead wood, recycling nutrients back to soil. Attract predatory beetles and woodpeckers that excavate trees to hunt them. Each tunnel system represents multiple generations.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Laid in tunnel galleries under bark

life_stages.name: Larva
life_stages.description: White C-shaped grubs tunneling through wood

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Small brown beetles; exit trees leaving tiny holes

image.author: Ron Long, Simon Fraser University, Bugwood.org

species.functional_description: Medium to large spiders that construct intricate geometric orb webs to trap flying insects. Webs rebuilt nightly at dusk; abandoned sections removed and eaten (protein recycling). Excellent hunters catching flies, midges, small moths, and other flying insects. Coloration camouflages them in webs during day. Favorite food of nuthatches, flycatchers, and wasps. Females lay egg sacs that overwinter in protected sites.

life_stages.name: Egg sac
life_stages.description: Protected overwinter case on protected surfaces

life_stages.name: Spiderling
life_stages.description: Tiny spiders emerging in spring; ballooning on silk threads

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Building webs in gardens, under eaves, and between plants

species.functional_description: Most common frog in NE PA ponds and stream edges. Males have bright green upper lip and prominent dorsolateral ridges — distinguishing it from Bullfrog. Territorial males produce banjo-twang calls from pond edges April–August. Bioindicator of water quality — absent from acidified or polluted water. Eats invertebrates, small fish, and other frogs; prey of herons, mink, snapping turtles, and large fish.

life_stages.name: Egg mass
life_stages.description: Surface film of up to 4,000 eggs on still water

life_stages.name: Tadpole
life_stages.description: Brown-green; overwinters one year before metamorphosis

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Green or brown with dorsolateral ridges; on pond edges and banks

species.functional_description: Most abundant turtle in NE PA. Flat shell with red-orange markings on edges and yellow stripes on head and neck. Basks in rows on logs and rocks — conspicuous summer indicator of wetland health. Omnivorous: eats algae, aquatic plants, invertebrates, carrion, and small fish. Nests June–July in upland soil near water. Hatchlings may overwinter in nest, tolerating partial freezing. Strongly associated with beaver ponds.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: 2–5 leathery eggs buried in upland soil; June–July

life_stages.name: Hatchling
life_stages.description: Coin-sized; may overwinter in nest and emerge in spring

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Basking in rows on logs; active in ponds and beaver impoundments

species.functional_description: Common, aggressive dragonfly of NE PA ponds. Males vivid powder-blue; females bright green. Fiercely territorial — chases other dragonflies including much larger species. Adults eat mosquitoes, midges, and small flying insects. Aquatic nymphs (2–3 years) are top invertebrate predators in ponds, eating tadpoles, mosquito larvae, and small invertebrates. Both larval and adult stages provide significant pest control.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Scattered on water surface by female in flight

life_stages.name: Nymph
life_stages.description: Aquatic; bottom-dweller in ponds; predatory — 2–3 year development

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Male vivid blue; female solid green; perching on vegetation at pond edge

species.functional_description: Tall emergent wetland plant forming dense colonies in shallow water. Distinctive brown hot-dog seed heads visible year-round. Provides critical nesting cover for Red-winged Blackbird, Marsh Wren, and Common Yellowthroat. Rhizomes and seed fluff used by Muskrat and Beaver for food and lodge material. Each seed head releases up to 220,000 wind-dispersed seeds. Beaver pond creation dramatically expands cattail stands — the two species form a positive feedback loop.
species.keystone_description: Foundation species of NE PA freshwater marshes. Dense stands are obligate nesting habitat for marsh-breeding birds (Red-winged Blackbird, Marsh Wren, Common Yellowthroat). Root rhizomes are primary winter food for Muskrat. Beaver pond creation and cattail expansion are tightly linked — each amplifying the other.

image.author: Peter O'Connor aka anemoneprojectors from Stevenage, United Kingdom

species.functional_description: Large (7–9 in) black salamander with two rows of bright yellow spots. Lives almost entirely underground in forest floor. Emerges only on the first warm rainy nights of late February–March to breed in vernal pools — one of NE PA's most iconic wildlife events. Egg masses harbor symbiotic green algae (Oophila amblystomatis) that oxygenates the eggs. Adults return underground immediately after breeding; rarely seen otherwise. Eats earthworms, slugs, and soil invertebrates.

life_stages.name: Egg mass
life_stages.description: Jelly mass with green algae; in vernal pools; Feb–Mar

life_stages.name: Larva
life_stages.description: Aquatic with external gills; in vernal pool; Apr–Jul

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Black with yellow spots; underground most of year; visible only on first warm spring rains

species.functional_description: Sleek, dark-brown semi-aquatic mustelid. Excellent swimmer; hunts along stream banks and pond edges. Eats frogs, fish, crayfish, muskrat, ducks, and small mammals. Dens in muskrat lodges and stream bank burrows. Year-round resident; active in winter under ice. Strongly associated with Beaver pond complexes and healthy stream corridors — presence indicates good water quality and prey base.

taxonomic_groups.label: Herons & Egrets
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Wading birds; stalk and strike prey in shallow water; colonial nesters in heronries

taxonomic_groups.label: Woodpeckers
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Bark foragers; excavate wood for food and nesting; primary and secondary cavity creators

taxonomic_groups.label: Oaks
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Mast-producing trees; support 557+ Lepidoptera species; foundational forest structure

taxonomic_groups.label: Milkweeds
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Cardenolide-producing host plants for Monarch and milkweed guild specialists; nectar sources

taxonomic_groups.label: Willows
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Early-blooming riparian trees and shrubs; beaver food source; support 289+ Lepidoptera species

taxonomic_groups.label: Frogs
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Aquatic breeders; bioindicators of water quality; chorus callers in spring; pond and wetland predators
taxonomic_groups.notes: Order Anura; families Ranidae and Hylidae

taxonomic_groups.label: Carrot-Family Plants
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Flat-topped flower clusters (umbels); obligate hosts for Black Swallowtail larvae at family level

taxonomic_groups.label: Native Fruiting Shrubs
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Berry-producing native shrubs; bird-dispersed seeds; critical fall and winter food for frugivores
taxonomic_groups.notes: Various families; includes Amelanchier, Cornus, Viburnum, Sambucus, Ilex, Vaccinium

taxonomic_groups.label: Songbirds
taxonomic_groups.common_traits: Perching birds with complex vocalizations; diverse diets; important seed dispersers and insect controllers
taxonomic_groups.notes: Order Passeriformes; includes Cardinals, Jays, Nuthatches, Flycatchers, and other perching birds

symbiosis.notes: Monarch caterpillars obligate on genus Asclepias. A. syriaca is preferred oviposition plant in NE PA due to moderate cardenolide levels.
Butterfly Weed is used by Monarch larvae — lower latex than A. syriaca.
Swamp Milkweed is a high-quality Monarch larval host in wet habitats.
Monarchs nectar heavily on goldenrod during fall migration — critical fat-building fuel before Mexico journey.
Red Milkweed Beetle obligate on genus Asclepias. Adults on foliage/flowers; larvae develop in roots — unique in Cerambycidae.
T. melanurus (tuberosa specialist) and occasionally T. tetrophthalmus use Butterfly Weed.
Large Milkweed Bug specialist on Asclepias seeds. Also feeds on pods, flowers, and vegetative tissue. Sequesters cardenolides.
Feeds on Butterfly Weed seeds and pods.
Feeds on Swamp Milkweed seeds.
Bumble bees are primary diurnal pollinators of Common Milkweed — produce 8x more mature pods than nocturnal moth visitors per Fritz & Morse 1981.
Bumble bees and butterflies pollinate Butterfly Weed, which lacks milky sap barrier.
B. impatiens and B. griseocollis are primary pollinators of Swamp Milkweed.
Goldenrod is critical fall pollen/nectar source for bumble bee gynes building fat reserves for winter. 43 Solidago specialist bee species.
Long-tongued bumble bee workers reach nectar in columbine spurs. Short-tongued bees rob through spur holes.
Black Willow catkins (March–April) are critical first-of-spring pollen and nectar for emerging bumble bee queens before other plants open.
Bumble bees are primary pollinators of Spicebush early spring flowers alongside Andrena bees and small flies.

Ruby-throated Hummingbird is primary pollinator of Eastern Columbine — deposits 10x more pollen per visit than bees per University of Maryland Extension. Bloom timing co-evolved with hummingbird spring arrival.
Spicebush Swallowtail obligate at Lauraceae family. Primary PA hosts are Spicebush and Sassafras. Larvae build silk-sealed folded-leaf shelters.
Black Cherry is the primary PA host plant for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail caterpillars.
White Oak is a facultative host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail — used alongside many other woody plants.
Red Oak is a facultative host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail.
Black Willow is a facultative host for Eastern Tiger Swallowtail and supports several other swallowtail species.
Black Swallowtail is obligate at family Apiaceae. Uses native Golden Alexanders, water hemlock, water parsnip, and naturalized/cultivated Queen Anne's lace, parsley, dill, fennel.
Black Willow is a primary food tree for Beaver — bark and cambium eaten; tree felled for dam/lodge construction.
Beaver uses all native willow species for food and dam construction material.
Beaver ponds and adjacent wetlands are primary foraging habitat for Great Blue Heron in NE PA. Beaver activity creates the shallow productive water herons depend on.
Great Blue Heron is a primary predator of American Bullfrog adults and large tadpoles.
Pileated Woodpecker excavates cavities used by Great Horned Owl for nesting.
American Kestrel uses abandoned Pileated Woodpecker cavities and Northern Flicker holes for nesting; also uses nest boxes.
Great Horned Owl is the primary natural predator of Red-tailed Hawk — takes eggs, nestlings, and occasionally adults.
Eastern Gray Squirrel is a major prey item for Red-tailed Hawk in forested PA landscapes.
Red-tailed Hawk occasionally takes White-tailed Deer fawns, though this is uncommon.
Eastern Gray Squirrel is a significant prey item for Great Horned Owl, especially in winter.
Great Horned Owl takes large frogs including Bullfrog adults near beaver ponds and wetlands.
Gray Squirrel preferentially caches and eats White Oak acorns (lower tannins, less bitter than red oak). A critical oak-regeneration mutualism — forgotten caches germinate into new trees.
Gray Squirrel caches Red Oak acorns — nips embryo tip before burial (preventing germination) then retrieves them. Red Oak acorns cached longer than white oak.
Gray Squirrel uses all native oak species — primary mast consumer and seed disperser across all Quercus.
White-tailed Deer eats White Oak acorns intensively in fall, and browses seedlings year-round — overabundant deer suppresses oak regeneration.
Deer browse Spicebush stems and leaves — heavy deer pressure can suppress Spicebush in understory, reducing Spicebush Swallowtail habitat.
Black Cherry is a major Cedar Waxwing food source — flocks strip cherries in August. Waxwings pass seeds through gut intact, dispersing them widely.
High-fat Spicebush berries (female plants only) are critical fall food for Cedar Waxwings and migrant thrushes.
Cedar Waxwing is a generalist frugivore of all native berry-producing shrubs — serviceberry, viburnum, dogwood, elderberry, winterberry. Major seed disperser.
Eastern Bluebird eats Black Cherry fruit in late summer and fall.
Bluebirds shift to native berries (viburnum, sumac, bayberry, holly) in winter when insects unavailable.
American Kestrel occasionally preys on Eastern Bluebird, especially where both use open-country nest boxes.
Downy Woodpecker excavates Eurosta gall fly ball-galls on goldenrod stems in winter to eat larvae inside — a critical food-web link between goldenrod and forest birds.
Common Yellowthroat catches butterflies and other flying insects including occasional Monarch adults.
All native PA Asclepias species — Poke Milkweed (forest shade), Whorled Milkweed (dry open), Four-leaved (woodland) — support Monarch larvae.
Eastern Tiger Swallowtail uses multiple oak species as facultative larval hosts beyond White and Red Oak.
Wood Duck nests in cavities excavated by Pileated Woodpecker. Woodpecker provides critical cavity homes in dead trees where wood ducks depend for successful breeding.

Northern Flying Squirrels nest in Pileated Woodpecker cavities. These large cavities (1.5 inches diameter) are critical winter shelter for flying squirrels — key to their survival in northern PA winters.
Black Cherry is the primary PA host for Eastern Tent Caterpillar. Silk tents in spring branches are conspicuous ecosystem indicator. Mass outbreaks attract numerous predators and parasitoids.
Black Willow is a facultative host for Spicebush Swallowtail caterpillars alongside primary Lauraceae family hosts (Spicebush, Sassafras). Larvae build silk-sealed folded-leaf shelters.

Blue Jay is the primary long-distance disperser of White Oak acorns in NE PA. Carries up to 5 acorns in expandable throat pouch; transports them up to 1 mile from source trees. Forgotten caches germinate into new trees, regenerating oak stands across fragmented landscape (Pesendorfer et al. 2016).

Blue Jay caches Red Oak acorns over longer distances than squirrels. High-tannin acorns are stored deeper and longer — increasing germination probability. Blue Jays select canopy gaps and forest edges as cache sites, preferentially restoring fragmented stands.

Blue Jays are the single most important long-distance oak disperser in eastern NA — responsible for rapid post-glacial oak reforestation and ongoing regeneration of isolated forest patches in today's fragmented landscape. Dispersal distances (up to 1+ mile) far exceed any other acorn-caching animal.
Great Blue Heron is a primary predator of Green Frog adults along pond and stream edges.
Great Blue Heron occasionally takes juvenile Painted Turtles; adults too large to handle.
Mink is a significant predator of American Bullfrog along beaver ponds and stream edges; hunts at night along shorelines.
Mink preys on Wood Duck eggs and ducklings; a natural predation pressure on cavity-nesting ducks in beaver pond habitat.
Green Frog is a common prey item for Mink along pond margins and stream banks.

Beaver pond creation floods low-lying areas, dramatically expanding the shallow standing water that Common Cattail requires. Cattail colonies in turn stabilize pond edges and provide lodge building material for Beaver — a positive feedback loop driving wetland expansion.

Adult Eastern Pondhawks are prey for Green Frogs and American Bullfrogs along pond edges. Pondhawk nymphs in turn eat small tadpoles and aquatic invertebrates — a reverse predation dynamic across life stages.
Common Cattail stands bordering vernal pools provide overwintering cover and humid terrestrial microhabitat for Spotted Salamanders migrating between forest and breeding pools.
